From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Squash championship held in Hopton-on-Sea, Great Yarmouth, Norfolk

| 2021 World Indoor [Bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls") Championship | |
| --- | --- |
| 43rd World Indoor Bowls Championship | |
| Location | [England](/wiki/England "England") [Hopton-on-Sea](/wiki/Hopton-on-Sea "Hopton-on-Sea"), [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") |
| Date | 8–24 January 2021 |
| Category | [World Indoor Championships](/wiki/World_Indoor_Bowls_Championships "World Indoor Bowls Championships") |
| ← [2020](/wiki/2020_World_Indoor_Bowls_Championship "2020 World Indoor Bowls Championship")  [2022](/wiki/2022_World_Indoor_Bowls_Championship "2022 World Indoor Bowls Championship") → | |

The **2021 Just World Indoor Bowls Championship** took place at [Potters Leisure Resort](/wiki/Potters_Resort "Potters Resort"), [Hopton-on-Sea](/wiki/Hopton-on-Sea "Hopton-on-Sea"), [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth"), [England](/wiki/England "England"), from 8–24 January 2021.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The event was organised by the [World Bowls Tour](/wiki/World_Bowls_Tour "World Bowls Tour"), televised by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") and streamed live on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The event was played [behind closed doors](/wiki/Behind_closed_doors_(sport) "Behind closed doors (sport)") and there was no under-25 competition due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[3]](#cite_note-3) As a consequence of the pandemic there were no overseas players involved.[[4]](#cite_note-PH-4)

A fairer system was devised in the Open pairs whereby all competitors would face a first round match and there would be no byes.[[4]](#cite_note-PH-4)

Late changes had to be made to the draw, with world number one and 2nd seed [Nick Brett](/wiki/Nick_Brett "Nick Brett") unable to participate because he broke his arm following an accident playing golf.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Scottish 11th seed [Darren Burnett](/wiki/Darren_Burnett "Darren Burnett") also pulled out through injury, while [Colin Walker](/wiki/Colin_Walker_(bowls) "Colin Walker (bowls)"), Ian Honnor and [Caroline Brown](/wiki/Caroline_Brown_(bowls) "Caroline Brown (bowls)") withdrew for personal reasons. Brown was replaced by [Rebecca Willgress](/wiki/Rebecca_Field_(bowls) "Rebecca Field (bowls)"). This also meant [Darren Weir](/wiki/Darren_Weir "Darren Weir"), [Julie Forrest](/wiki/Julie_Forrest "Julie Forrest"), [Katherine Rednall](/wiki/Katherine_Rednall "Katherine Rednall") and [Ellen Falkner](/wiki/Ellen_Falkner "Ellen Falkner") were added to the Open Singles field, with Weir, Rednall, Falkner and [Devon Cooper](/wiki/Devon_Cooper "Devon Cooper") being added into the Open Pairs.

In the Men's Singles, the quarter-final match between [Perry Martin](/wiki/Perry_Martin_(bowls) "Perry Martin (bowls)") and [Wayne Willgress](/wiki/Wayne_Willgress "Wayne Willgress") was declared void following a positive [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") test for both players. As a result [Mark Dawes](/wiki/Mark_Dawes "Mark Dawes") (who was due to play the winner in the semi-finals) received a walkover into the final.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The other finalist was [Greg Harlow](/wiki/Greg_Harlow "Greg Harlow") who was a comfortable winner over [David Gourlay](/wiki/David_Gourlay "David Gourlay") in the only semi final. In the final Dawes controlled the match and won 10-3 11-5 to claim his second singles title.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the Women's Singles, [Laura Daniels](/wiki/Laura_Daniels "Laura Daniels") claimed her second title success by beating Katherine Rednall in a repeat of the 2015 final in which the Welshwoman also won. Rednall, seven months pregnant, had to compete just minutes after beating [Alex Marshall](/wiki/Alex_Marshall_(bowls) "Alex Marshall (bowls)") in the second round of the Open Singles. Earlier in the competition, Ellen Falkner had to withdraw from her first round match with Rednall due to a shoulder injury. Defending champion Julie Forrest went out in the first round, beaten by Devon Cooper.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the Open Pairs, Ellen Falkner partnered by Greg Harlow (the defending champion) became the first woman in the competition's history to reach an open final[[11]](#cite_note-11) where they met [2018](/wiki/2018_World_Indoor_Bowls_Championship "2018 World Indoor Bowls Championship") champions Mark Dawes & [Jamie Chestney](/wiki/Jamie_Chestney "Jamie Chestney"). Harlow and Falkner won a tight first set 7-6 with Harlow consistently drawing to win an end. Dawes playing a strong lead helped secure a comfortable second set 9-2, sending the match into a tie break. Dawes and Chestney won the tie break 2-1.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the Mixed Pairs final [Stewart Anderson](/wiki/Stewart_Anderson_(bowls) "Stewart Anderson (bowls)") and Julie Forrest defeated [Robert Paxton](/wiki/Robert_Paxton_(bowls) "Robert Paxton (bowls)") and [Carla Banks](/wiki/Carla_Banks "Carla Banks").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
